meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve can't finish projects (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome can't finish projects. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve Project Abandonment
content.seo_description: Stop abandoning projects. Learn how to implement SaaS pricing as a forcing function for completion. A beginner guide using Antigravity and Claude Code.
content.answer_block: Project abandonment stems from a lack of stakes. By integrating a SaaS pricing model early, you transform a 'hobby project' into a 'real-world business.' This shift creates technical constraints and accountability, forcing you to prioritize core features, finish your MVP, and finally ship a product that provides actual value.
content.intro:
You’ve been here before: the initial excitement of a new app idea, the clean slate of a fresh repository, and the inevitable downward spiral into feature creep. By week three, the motivation wanes, and your project becomes another abandoned folder on your desktop—a graveyard of half-finished dreams and 'work-in-progress' README files.

But what if you treated your code like a business from day one? The secret to finishing isn't more willpower; it's the constraint of commerce. When you commit to a pricing model, you stop building 'everything' and start building 'the solution.' This case study shows you how to use Antigravity and Claude Code to bypass the fluff and ship your first professional SaaS.

steps.title: Define the Value Metric
steps.description: Don't build features; build a solution someone pays for. Define a single, measurable unit of value (e.g., 'per report generated').
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze my project idea and suggest a value-based pricing model that limits scope to a single, high-value core feature.
steps.claude_code_command: claude /ask 'Based on my project description, what is the single most valuable feature I can monetize to prevent scope creep?'

steps.title: Architect for Paid Tiers
steps.description: Structure your database and authentication to support multi-tenancy. This forces you to handle data isolation early.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a database schema for a SaaS app that supports a free tier and a premium subscription using a multi-tenant architecture.
steps.claude_code_command: claude /edit 'Update the current database schema to include a subscription_status and plan_id field on the user model.'

steps.title: Implement Stripe Checkout Flow
steps.description: Stop mocking payments. Integrate a real checkout flow early to make the project feel tangible and real.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Provide a clean, secure implementation pattern for Stripe Checkout in a Next.js/Node.js environment.
steps.claude_code_command: claude /run 'Create a Stripe webhook handler to update user subscription status in the database upon successful payment.'

steps.title: Build the Paywall Guard
steps.description: Implement middleware that restricts access based on subscription status. This is the 'finish line' for your MVP.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a middleware function that checks the user's subscription status and redirects unauthorized users to the pricing page.
steps.claude_code_command: claude /edit 'Apply a middleware guard to all routes inside /app/dashboard to ensure only paid users can access core features.'

steps.title: Deploy and Validate
steps.description: Push to production. A live URL changes your psychology from 'student' to 'founder'.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Provide a checklist for a production-ready SaaS launch, focusing on security and environment variable management.
steps.claude_code_command: claude /run 'Configure the deployment pipeline to include production environment variables and sanity check the build command.'

pro_tips: Always build the paywall before the features; it forces you to prioritize what actually matters.
Use Stripe's Test Mode to simulate edge cases like subscription failures to harden your app's error handling.
Limit your MVP to one specific persona; if you try to sell to everyone, you will build nothing.
Treat your README.md as a sales page; if you can't explain why someone should pay, you aren't ready to code it.
Automate your feedback loop by embedding a 'Report a Bug' button that routes directly to your email.
content.next_steps: Once your paywall is live, focus on getting your first five users. Don't worry about scaling; worry about solving one real problem for one real person.
content.conversion_block: You now have the technical blueprint to turn your unfinished code into a professional SaaS product. Stop dreaming and start executing with the tools that bridge the gap between student and pro. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.

acceptedAnswer.text: Don't build features; build a solution someone pays for. Define a single, measurable unit of value (e.g., 'per report generated').
Structure your database and authentication to support multi-tenancy. This forces you to handle data isolation early.
Stop mocking payments. Integrate a real checkout flow early to make the project feel tangible and real.
Implement middleware that restricts access based on subscription status. This is the 'finish line' for your MVP.
Push to production. A live URL changes your psychology from 'student' to 'founder'.
